13.6. INTERMEDIATE VALUE THEOREM 
By the Intermediate Value Theorem, a continuous f maps the compact interval [a; b] into the compact interval [m; M ]. We can express this property by saying that the continuous image of a compact interval is a compact interval. Momentarily, we will extend this property to any interval, compact or not (Proposition 593). 

Bolzano’s Theorem is, via Lemma 587, behind the Intermediate Value Theorem. Its statement is a special case. Indeed, observe that its hypothesis f (a) � f (b) � 0 can be equivalently stated as 

Clearly, 

and so the Intermediate Value Theorem ensures the existence of c 2 [a; b] such that f (c) = 0. 

The continuity of f on [a; b] is crucial for the Intermediate Value Theorem. To see this, consider for example the so-called signum function sgn : R ! R de…ned by 

Its restriction sgn : [�1; 1] ! R on the interval [�1; 1] is continuous at all the points of this interval except at the origin, where it has a non-removable jump discontinuity. So, the continuity hypothesis of the Intermediate Value Theorem does not hold. The image of sgn x consists of only three points f�1; 0; 1g, with m = �1 and M = 1. For every z 2 [�1; 1] with z = �1; 0; 1 there is no x 2 [�1; 1] such that sgn x = z. 

## 13.6.2 Some remarkable consequences 

A nice consequence of the Intermediate Value Theorem is a characterization of scalar continuous injective functions that completes what we established in Proposition 221. In the rest of this section I denotes any interval, bounded or not, of the real line. 

Proposition 589 A continuous function f : I ! R is injective if and only if it is strictly monotone. 

The proof of this result relies on a lemma that presents a useful characterization of strict monotonicity on open intervals. 

Lemma 590 A function f : (a; b) ! R, with a; b 2 R[�] , is strictly monotone if and only if 

for all x; y; z 2 (a; b). 

CHAPTER 13. CONTINUOUS FUNCTIONS 
Strict monotonicity thus holds if and only if x < z < y implies either f (x) < f (z) < f (y) or f (y) < f (z) < f (x). 

Proof “If”. Let x; y; z 2 (a; b) be such that x < z < y. If f is strictly monotone, then f (x) < f (z) < f (y), and so min ff (x) ; f (y)g = f (x) < f (z) < f (y) = max ff (x) ; f (y)g. “Only if”. Suppose, per contra, that f is not strictly monotone. Then, there exist x; y; w; z 2 (a; b) such that

As �< w < z, by (13.21) we have

which contradicts (13.22). � 

Proof of Proposition 589 The “if” follows from Proposition 221. As to the converse, assume that f is injective. We want to show that f is strictly monotone. It is enough to show that f is strictly monotone on int I since the strict monotonicity on I then follows from the continuity of f (why?). So, let us assume that I is an open interval. Suppose, by contradiction, that f is not strictly monotone. By the last lemma, there exist x < z < y such that either f (z) � max ff (x) ; f (y)g or f (z) � min ff (x) ; f (y)g. As f is injective, these inequalities are actually strict. Suppose that f (z) > max ff (x) ; f (y)g, the other case being similarly handled. Let f (z) > k > max ff (x) ; f (y)g. That is, we have both f (z) > k > f (x) and f (z) > k > f (y). We conclude that f is strictly monotone. �

Next we show that the continuous image of an interval is an interval, thus extending to any interval what the Intermediate Value Theorem ensured for compact intervals. 

Proposition 593 If a function f : I ! R is continuous, its image Im f is an interval. 

Proof Let t1; t2 2 Im f , say t1 < t2, and � 2 [0; 1]. We want to show that �t1 + (1 � �) t2 2 Im f . There exist x1; x2 2 I such that f (x1) = t1 and f (x2) = t2. Let m = minx2[x1;x2] f (x) and M = maxx2[x1;x2] f (x). In view of (13.19), by the Intermediate Value Theorem we have f ([x1; x2]) = [m; M ]. Since t1; t2 2 f ([x1; x2]), we thus have �t1 + (1 � �) t2 2 f ([x1; x2]), and so �t1 + (1 � �) t2 2 Im f . This proves that Im f is an interval. � 

This “interval image”property actually characterizes continuity for monotone functions de…ned on intervals. Intuitively, monotone functions may have only jump discontinuities (Proposition 575), a possibility that an interval image precludes. 

Proposition 594 A monotone function f : I ! R is continuous if and only if its image Im f is an interval. 

Without monotonicity this elegant result fails: the image of the discontinuous and nonmonotone function f : R ! R given by 

is the unbounded interval R.

On the other hand, it holds f (x0 � ") < a < b < f (x0 + ") and so, being Im f an interval, 

This contradiction proves that f is continuous at x0 2 int I. We leave to the reader the similar argument for the lower endpoint x0 = min I of the interval I (when relevant, i.e., when min I 2 I) and for its upper endpoint x0 = max I (when relevant, i.e., when max I 2 I). �

## 13.6.3 Multivariable version 

Using convex sets, which generalize intervals to R[n] , we have the following extension of Proposition 593 to operators.[16] 

Proposition 596 Let C be a convex set in R[n] . If an operator f : C ! R[m] is continuous, its image Im f is a convex set in R[m] . 

In words, the continuous image of a convex set is convex. 

Proof Let t1; t2 2 Im f , say t1 < t2, and � 2 [0; 1]. We want to show that �t1 + (1 � �) t2 2 Im f . There exist x1; x2 2 C such that f (x1) = t1 and f (x2) = t2. So, by Proposition 593 its image Im ' is an interval. Thus, �t1 + (1 � �) t2 2 Im ', so there exists �� 2 [0; 1] such that f (��x1 + (1 � ��) x2) = ' (��) = �t1 +(1 � �) t2. This implies that �t1 +(1 � �) t2 2 Im f , as desired. � 

When m = 1, a simple consequence of this result is the following multivariable extension of the Intermediate Value Theorem. Here K denotes a compact and convex set in R[n] .

## 13.7 Operators 

The notion of continuity extends in a natural way to operators. To see why, we …rst extend the notion of limit to operators and then use it to de…ne their continuity.

## 13.7.2 Continuity 

Armed with a notion of limit for operators, we can move to their continuity. 

De…nition 604 An operator f : A � R[n] ! R[m] is said to be continuous at a limit point x0 2 A if 

By convention, f is continuous at each isolated point of A. 

Here, too, an operator that is continuous at all the points of a subset E of the domain A is called continuous on E, while an operator that is continuous at all the points of its domain is called continuous. It is easy to see that the two operators of the last example are continuous. 

The "-� characterization of continuity for operators is readily obtained by replacing in (13.9) the absolute value inequality jf (x) � f (x0)j < " with the norm one kf (x) � f (x0)k < ". More importantly, we have the following componentwise characterization of continuity. 

Proposition 605 An operator is continuous at a point of its domain if and only if all its component functions are continuous there. 

CHAPTER 13. CONTINUOUS FUNCTIONS 
The continuity of an operator is thus brought back to the continuity of its component functions, resulting in a componentwise notion of continuity. We omit the proof as it is a straightforward consequence of Proposition 602, as the reader can check. 

Example 606 All the operators in Examples 599 and 600 are continuous because their component functions are continuous. For instance, f : R[2] ! R[2] de…ned by f (x1; x2) = (x1; x1x2) is continuous because its component functions f1; f2 : R[2] ! R given by f1 (x1; x2) = x1 and f2 (x1; x2) = x1x2 are both continuous. N 

The convergence of vectors is equivalent to that of their components (Section 8.16). This basic property will allow the reader to prove the next three-fold sequential characterization of continuity, which extends Proposition 570 to operators. In reading this result it is important to keep in mind that here f (xn) ! f (x0) indicates convergence of vectors in R[m] . 

Proposition 607 Let f : A � R[n] ! R[m] and x0 2 A. The following conditions are equivalent: 

This result permits to extend to operators the continuity results established for functions of several variables, except the ones that use in an essential way the order structure of their codomain R, like the Bolzano and Weierstrass Theorems. We leave to the reader such extensions. 

## 13.8 Infracoda topologica 

## 13.8.1 Preimages 

Implication (13.10) can be equivalently written as 

This observation suggests the following purely topological characterization of continuity.[20] 

Proposition 608 A function f : A � R[n] ! R is continuous at x0 2 A if and only if, for each open set V containing f (x0), there exists an open set U containing x0 such that f (U \ A) � V . 

> 20 We use the term “topological”because it is a property purely in terms of open sets. 

13.8. INFRACODA TOPOLOGICA 
Proof As neighborhoods are open sets, in view of Proposition 569 the “if” is obvious. As to the converse, take any V" (f (x0)) with " > 0. By hypothesis, there exists an open set U containing x0 such that f (U \ A) � V" (f (x0)). As x0 is an interior point of U , there exists a neighborhood U�" (x0) � U . Thus, f (U�" (x0) \ A) � V" (f (x0)), as desired. � 

This topological characterization leads, in turn, to a topological characterization of continuity in terms of preimages of open and closed sets. For simplicity, we …rst consider operators de…ned on the whole space. 

Proposition 609 An operator f : R[n] ! R[m] is continuous if and only if the preimages of closed sets are, in turn, closed. 

In symbols, the preimage f[�][1] (C) of each closed set C of R[m] is itself a closed set of R[n] . The proof of this proposition relies on some basic set-theoretic properties of images and preimages, whose proof is left to the reader. 

Lemma 610 Let f : X ! Y be a function between any two sets X and Y . We have: 

With this, we can turn to the proof. 

Proof of Proposition 609 “If”. Suppose that f is continuous. Let C be a closed set of R[m] . Let fxng � f[�][1] (C) be such that xn ! x0 2 R[n] . We want to show that x0 2 f[�][1] (C). Set yn = f (xn). Since f is continuous, we have f (xn) ! f (x0). Then f (x0) 2 C because C is closed. In turn, this implies x0 2 f[�][1] (C), as desired. “Only if”. Suppose that, for each closed set C of R[m] , the set f[�][1] (C) is closed in R[n] . So, if V is an open set in R[m] containing f (x0), the set f[�][1] (V ) is open in R[n] because �f[�][1] (V )�c = f �1 (V c). Thus, being x0 2 f �1 (V ), there exists a neighborhood B (x0) such that B (x0) � f[�][1] (V ). So, f (B (x0)) � f �f[�][1] (V )� � V . In view of (13.10), we conclude that f is continuous at x0. � 

Example 611 Level sets f[�][1] (y) = fx 2 R[n] : f (x) = yg of a continuous operator f : R[n] ! R[m] are closed sets since singletons fyg are closed sets in R[m] . N 

In view of Lemma 610-(ii), there is a dual version of the last proposition for open sets. Because of its importance, we report it formally.[21] 

Corollary 612 An operator f : R[n] ! R[m] is continuous if and only if the preimages of open sets are, in turn, open. 

> 21 This is, indeed, the characterization of continuity used to generalize this fundamental notion well beyond Euclidean spaces, as readers will learn in more advanced courses. 

CHAPTER 13. CONTINUOUS FUNCTIONS 
The next two examples illustrate the last two results. The …rst example records, in the positive, some interesting consequences, while the second one shows, in the negative, what may go wrong without continuity. 

Example 613 By Proposition 609, the upper and lower contour sets:[22] 

of a continuous function f : R[n] ! R are closed because the intervals [t; 1) and (1; t] are closed. By Corollary 612, their strict versions 

are open because the intervals (t; 1) and (1; t) are open.

13.8. INFRACODA TOPOLOGICA 
The ‡oor function is thus an example of an increasing function with countably many discontinuities. More is true: the lower contour sets of the ‡oor function, rather than being closed as the ones of a continuous function (Example 613), are instead open intervals. In fact, for each t 2 R we have (f � t) = (�1; [t] + 1). For example, (f � 1) = (�1; 2). 

By replacing x with [x] as an argument, we can “screw”a continuous function g : R ! R by injecting countably many discontinuities. Speci…cally, take an integer z with g (z � 1) 6= g (z). The composition g � f : R ! R given by g ([x]) is discontinuous at z since 

For instance, for the downward parabola g (t) = 1 � t[2] we have g (z � 1) = g (z) for all integers z, and so the function g � f : R ! R given by 

is discontinuous at all integers. Its graph is: 

Observe that the upper contour sets are open. 

N 

Next we generalize the preimage characterization of continuity to general operators, not necessarily de…ned on the whole space. 

Proposition 615 An operator f : C ! R[m] de…ned on a closed subset of R[n] is continuous if and only if the preimages of closed sets are, in turn, closed. 

The function f : (0; 1] ! R given by f (x) = x readily shows that this proposition is false without the topological hypothesis on the domain. 

Proof “If”. Suppose that f is continuous. Let F be a closed set of R[m] . Let fxng � f[�][1] (F ) \ C be such that xn ! x0 2 R[n] . We want to show that x0 2 f[�][1] (F ) \ C. Since C is closed, we have x0 2 C. Set yn = f (xn). Since f is continuous on C, we have f (xn) ! f (x0). Then f (x0) 2 F because F is closed. In turn, this implies x0 2 f[�][1] (F ). We conclude that x0 2 f[�][1] (F ) \ C, as desired. 

“Only if”. Suppose that, for each closed set F of R[m] , the set f[�][1] (F ) \ C is closed in R[n] . So, if V is an open set in R[m] containing f (x0), the set f[�][1] (V ) [ C[c] is open in 

CHAPTER 13. CONTINUOUS FUNCTIONS 
R[n] because �f[�][1] (V )�c \ C = f �1 (V c) \ C. Thus, being x0 2 f[�][1] (V ), there exists a neighborhood B (x0) such that B (x0) � f[�][1] (V ) [ C[c] , i.e., B (x0) \ C � f[�][1] (V ) \ C.[23] So, f (B (x0) \ C) � f �f[�][1] (V ) \ C� � f �f[�][1] (V )� � V . In view of (13.10), we conclude that f is continuous at x0. � 

In a dual way, an operator de…ned on an open set is continuous if and only if its preimages are open. For later reference, we state this dual version formally. 

Corollary 616 An operator f : U ! R[m] de…ned on an open subset of R[n] is continuous if and only if the preimages of open sets are, in turn, open. 

## 13.8.2 Images 

There is no counterpart of the preimage characterization for images: for a continuous function, in general the image of an open (closed) set is not open (closed). In other words, the continuous image of an open (closed) set is not necessarily open (closed). 

Example 617 (i) Let f : R ! R be the quadratic function f (x) = x[2] . For the open interval I = (�1; 1) we have f (I) = [0; 1), which is not open. 

(ii) Let f : R ! R be the exponential function f (x) = e[x] . The real line R is a closed set (also open, but here this is not of interest), but f (R) = (0; 1) is not closed. N 

The next result clari…es why in (ii) we have a closed but unbounded (so not compact) set like R. 

Proposition 618 Let K be a compact set in R[n] . If f : K ! R[m] is continuous, its image Im f is a compact set in R[m] . 

In words, the continuous image of a compact set is compact. 

Proof With the notions of topology at our disposal we are able to prove the result only in the case n = m = 1 (the general case, however, does not present substantial di¤erences). So, let n = m = 1. To show that the set Im f is bounded in R it is necessary to show that it is bounded both above and below in R (cf. De…nition 32). Suppose, by contradiction, that Im f is unbounded above. Then there exists a sequence fyng � Im f such that limn!1 yn = +1. Let fxng � K be the corresponding sequence with f (xn) = yn for every n. The sequence fxng is bounded since it is contained in the bounded set K. By the Bolzano-Weierstrass ~ ~ Theorem, there exist a subsequence fxnk g and a point x 2 R such that limk!1 xnk = x. ~ Since K is closed, we have x 2 K. Moreover, the continuity of f implies limk!1 ynk = limk!1 f (xnk ) = f (~x) 2 R. This contradicts limk!1 ynk = limn!1 yn = +1. It follows that the set Im f is bounded above in the real line. In a similar way, one shows that it is also bounded below. Thus, the set Im f is bounded in the real line. 

To complete the proof that Im f is compact, it remains to show that it is closed. Consider a sequence fyng � Im f that converges to y 2 R. By Theorem 176, we must show that y 2 Im f . Since fyng � Im f , by de…nition there exists a sequence fxng � K such that f (xn) = yn. As seen above, the sequence fxng is bounded. The Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem yields 

> 23 Recall property (1.4). 

13.9. CODA UNIFORME 
~ ~ ~ a subsequence fxnk g and a point x 2 R such that limk!1 xnk = x. Since K is closed, x 2 K. Moreover, the continuity of f implies that 

Therefore, y 2 Im f , as desired. � 

The fact that continuity preserves compactness is quite remarkable. It is another characteristic that distinguishes compact sets among closed sets, for which this property in general fails, as Example 617-(ii) shows. 

A nice dividend of this proposition is an operator version of Proposition 591 about the continuity of inverses. 

Corollary 619 Let K be a compact set in R[n] . A continuous bijective function f : K ! R[m] has continuous inverse f[�][1] : Im f ! R[n] . 

Proof Also here we consider the scalar case m = n = 1. By Proposition 618, the set Im f is compact. Let C be a closed subset of Im f . We want to show that f[�][1] (C) is closed. Since Im f is compact, C is actually compact. Let fxng � f[�][1] (C) be a sequence that converges to x 2 R[n] . We want to show that x 2 f[�][1] (C). By de…nition of f[�][1] (C), there exists a sequence fyng � C such that yn = f (xn) for each n � 1. Since C is compact, by the Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem there exists a subsequence fynk g that converges to some y 2 C. By the continuity of f , we conclude that x = f[�][1] (y) 2 f[�][1] (C), thus proving that f[�][1] (C) is closed. So, f[�][1] maps closed sets into closed sets. In view of Proposition 615 (momentarily established), this proves that f[�][1] is continuous. � 

We let readers to contrast this corollary with Proposition 591. Meditations will be helped by the next example that, along with the earlier Example 592, shows the importance of a compact domain for the last result and of an interval domain for that proposition.

## 13.9 Coda uniforme 

Besides on ", the value of �" in the epsilon-delta condition (13.9) depends also on the point x0 at hand, a fact so obvious that it was not even worth mentioning when discussing continuity. In this coda we focus on this property because an important, stronger, notion of continuity for a function f arises when, given any " > 0, we can choose the same �" for every point x0 2 A. That is, once " is …xed, the same �" works at all the points of the domain of f . 

CHAPTER 13. CONTINUOUS FUNCTIONS 
De…nition 621 A function f : A � R[n] ! R is said to be uniformly continuous if, for every " > 0, there exists �" > 0 such that 

Here the value of �" depends only on ", no longer on a point x0 of A. So, this value of �" applies uniformly across all points of the domain. Indeed, no speci…c point x0 is mentioned in this de…nition, which considers only the domain of the function per se. 

Uniform continuity is a remarkable property that permits to “control” the distance jf (x) � f (y)j between images via the distance jx � yj between arguments. It implies continuity, but the converse does not hold. For example, we will see soon that the quadratic function is continuous, but not uniformly. Yet, the two notions of continuity become equivalent on compact sets, a further remarkable property of this class of sets. 

Theorem 622 Let K be a compact set in R[n] . A function f : K ! R is continuous if and only if it is uniformly continuous. 

Proof The “if”is obvious because uniform continuity implies continuity. We prove the “only if”. For simplicity, consider the scalar case n = 1 with K = [a; b]. So, let f : [a; b] ! R be continuous. We need to show that it is also uniformly continuous. Suppose per contra that there exist " > 0 and two sequences fxng and fyng in [a; b] with jxn � ynj ! 0 and 

Since the sequences fxng and fyng are bounded, the Bolzano-Weierstrass’Theorem yields two convergent subsequences fxnk g and fynk g, i.e., there exist x; y 2 [a; b] such that xnk ! x and ynk ! y. Since xn�yn ! 0, we have xnk �ynk ! 0 and, therefore, x�y = 0 because of the uniqueness of the limit. Since f is continuous, we have f (xnk ) ! f (x) and f (ynk ) ! f (y). Hence, f (xnk ) � f (ynk ) ! f (x) � f (y) = 0, which contradicts (13.23). We conclude that f is uniformly continuous. � 

Theorem 622 does not hold without assuming the compactness of K, as the next counterexamples show. The …rst one considers a closed but unbounded set –the real line –while the other ones consider bounded sets which are not closed. 

Example 623 The quadratic function f (x) = x[2] is continuous but not uniformly continuous. Suppose, by contradiction, that it is uniformly continuous on R. By setting " = 1, there exists �" > 0 such that

Therefore, the quadratic function is not uniformly continuous. Yet, its restriction to any compact interval [a; b] is uniformly continuous thanks to the last theorem. We can check this directly, for instance, for the restriction f : [0; 1] ! R on the closed unit interval. We have

## 13.10.1 Stone-Weierstrass’Theorem 

Polynomials are the simplest continuous functions on the real line. Remarkably, any continuous function de…ned on a compact interval [a; b] of the real line may be approximated, arbitrarily well, by polynomials. 

CHAPTER 13. CONTINUOUS FUNCTIONS

This important result was proven by Karl Weierstrass in 1885, with a signi…cant extension due to Marshall Stone in 1937.[24] A third protagonist of this result is Sergei Bernstein, who in 1913 gave a beautiful proof of this theorem in which the approximating polynomials – aptly called Bernstein polynomials –are explicitly constructed when [a; b] is the unit interval [0; 1]. Here we will follow his lead by proving the Stone-Weierstrass Theorem via Bernstein’s result. 

Before doing that, however, we give a sandwich version of Stone-Weierstrass’Theorem that is sometimes useful.

## 14.1.1 Equations 

An operator f = (f1; :::; fn) : A � R[n] ! R[n] and a vector y 2 R[n] de…nes an (operator ) equation 

that is, 

The vector x is the unknown of the equation, while the vector y is the known term. A solution of equation (14.1) is a vector x 2 A such that f (x) = y. The preimage 

is thus the collection of all solutions. 

When y = 0, we say that the equation is homogeneous. By absorbing the known term in the operator, that is, by considering the operator g = f � y, we can reduce equation (14.1) to the homogeneous one g (x) = 0. Since 

to solve an equation for a given known term y amounts to solve its homogeneous version. For this reason, in the rest of the chapter we focus directly on the homogeneous equation

## 14.1.2 Poincaré-Miranda’s Theorem 

A fundamental issue in dealing with equations is the existence of solutions, that is, whether there exist vectors x 2 A such that f (x) = 0. As well-known from (at least) high school, this might well not be the case: with f : R ! R given by f (x) = x[2] + 1, there are no x 2 R such that x[2] + 1 = 0. 

Bolzano’s Theorem is a powerful result to establish the existence of solutions in the scalar case. Indeed, if f : A � R ! R is a continuous function and A is an interval, then equation 

has a solution provided there exist x[0] ; x[00] 2 A such that f (x[0] ) < 0 < f (x[00] ). For instance, in this way Corollary 580 was able to establish the existence of solutions of some polynomial equations. 

Bolzano’s Theorem admits a generalization to R[n] that, surprisingly, turns out to be a quite di¢ cult result, known as Poincaré-Miranda’s Theorem.[1] A piece of notation: given a vector x 2 R[n] , we write 

to emphasize the component i of vector x. For instance, if x = (4; 7; 11) then x1 = 4 and x�1 = (7; 11), while x3 = 11 and x�3 = (4; 7). 

Theorem 634 (Poincaré-Miranda) Let f = (f1; :::; fn) : [a; b] ! R[n] be a continuous operator de…ned on an interval of R[n] . If, for each i = 1; :::; n, we have 

then there exists c 2 [a; b] such that f (c) = 0.[2] 

> 1 It was stated in 1883 by Henri Poincaré and proved by Carlo Miranda in 1940 (unaware of Poincaré’s earlier work). For a proof, we refer interested readers to Kulpa (1997). 

2 3 For instance, if a; b 2 R , then [a�1; b�1] = [a2; b2] � [a3; b3], [a�2; b�2] = [a1; b1] � [a3; b3] and [a�3; b�3] = [a1; b1] � [a2; b2].

for each x 2 [a; b]. Since h is continuous, by the Poincaré-Miranda’s Theorem there exists c 2 [a; b] such that h (c) = 0, that is, f (c) = g (c). � 

Through this result we can generalize the equilibrium analysis that we carried out earlier in the book for the market of a single good (Proposition 584). Consider now a market where n goods are traded. Let 

CHAPTER 14. EQUATIONS AND FIXED POINTS 
be, respectively, the aggregate demand and supply functions, that is, at price p 2 [a; b] � R[n] + the market demands a quantity Di (p) � 0 and o¤ers a quantity Si (p) � 0 of each good i = 1; :::; n. 

A pair (p; q) 2 [a; b] � R[n] +[of][prices][and][quantities][is][a][market][equilibrium][if] 

The last result permits to establish the existence of such equilibrium, thus generalizing Proposition 584 to the general case of n goods. Besides continuity, existence requires that, for each good i, we have 

At its smallest possible price, a i, the demand of the good i is greater than its supply regardless of the prices of the other goods, while the opposite is true at its highest possible price bi. To …x ideas, assume that a = 0. Then, condition Di (0; p�i) � Si (0; p�i) just means that demand of a free good will always exceed its supply, regardless of which are the prices of the other goods (a reasonable assumption). In contrast, the opposite happens at the highest price bi, at which the supply of good i exceeds its demand regardless of the prices of the other goods (a reasonable assumption as long as bi is “high enough”). 

Via the excess demand function E : [a; b] ! R[n] de…ned by 

we can formulate the equilibrium condition (14.8) as a market equation 

For n = 1, it reduces to the earlier one-good market equation (13.18). A pair (p; q) of prices and quantities is a market equilibrium if and only if price p solves this equation and q = D (p). There is excess demand at price p of good i if Ei (p) � 0 and excess supply if Ei (p) � 0. In equilibrium, there is neither excess demand nor excess supply. Next we state the general equilibrium existence result in excess demand terms. 

Proposition 637 Let the excess demand function E : [a; b] ! R be continuous and such that, for each good i = 1; :::; n, 

Then, there exists a market equilibrium (p; q) 2 [a; b] � R[n] +[.] 

This result thus establishes the existence of equilibria under the reasonable assumptions previously discussed. It can be easily extended to the standard case when demand and supply functions are de…ned on R[n] +[,][so][have][the][form][D][:][R][n] +[!][R][n] +[and][S][:][R][n] +[!][R][n] +[,][by] requiring the existence of prices p[0] < p[00] that play the roles of the vectors a and b, respectively. These prices are here, mutatis mutandis, the analogs of the vectors x[0] and x[00] considered for equation (14.7). 

14.1. EQUATIONS 
## 14.1.3 Fixed points 

We can look at the scalar equation f (x) = 0 from a di¤erent angle. De…ne the auxiliary function g : A � R ! R by g (x) = �f (x) + x, with � = 0. A scalar x� 2 A solves the scalar equation if and only if g (�x) = x�. The scalar x� is said to be a …xed point of function g. So, a scalar is a solution of the equation de…ned by function f if and only if it is a …xed point of function g. Solving an equation thus amounts to …nd a …xed point. 

In the scalar case, this remark is just a bit more than a curiosum. In contrast, it becomes important in the general vector case because sometimes the best way to solve the general equation (14.1) is to consider an associated …xed point problem, so to reduce the solution of an equation to the search of the …xed points of suitable operators. For this reason in this section we study …xed points. 

An operator f : A � R[n] ! R[n] is said to be a self-map if f (A) � A, that is, if f (x) 2 A for all x 2 A. In words, self-maps associates to elements of A elements of A. They never escape A. To emphasize this key feature, we often write f : A ! A. 

Example 638 (i) All operators f : R[n] ! R[n] are, trivially, self-maps. (ii) The function f : [0; 1] ! R given by f (x) = x[2] is a self-map because x[2] 2 [0; 1] for all x 2 [0; 1]. In contrast, the function f : [0; 1] ! R given by f (x) = x + 1 is not a self-map because, for instance, f (1) = 2 2= [0; 1]. N 

Self-maps are important here because they may admit …xed points. 

De…nition 639 Given a self-map f : A ! A, a vector x� 2 A is said to be a …xed point of f � if f (�x) = x. 

For instance, for the quadratic self-map f : [0; 1] ! [0; 1] given by f (x) = x[2] , the endpoints 0 and 1 of the unit interval are …xed points. For the self-map f : R[2] ! R[2] given by f (x1; x2) = (x1; x1x2), the origin is a …xed point in that f (0) = 0. 

Turn now to the key question of the existence of …xed points. In the scalar case, it is an immediate consequence of Bolzano’s Theorem. 

Lemma 640 A continuous self-map f : [0; 1] ! [0; 1] has a …xed point. 

Proof The result is obviously true if either f (0) = 0 or f (1) = 1. Suppose f (0) > 0 and f (1) < 1. De…ne the auxiliary function g : [0; 1] ! R by g (x) = x � f (x). Then, g (0) < 0 � and g (1) > 0. Since g is continuous, by Bolzano’s Theorem there exists x 2 (0; 1) such that � � g (�x) = 0. Hence, f (�x) = x, and so x is a …xed point. � 

In the general case, the existence of …xed points is ensured by the famous Brouwer’s Fixed Point Theorem.[3] In analogy with the scalar case, it can be viewed as a consequence of the Poincaré-Miranda’s Theorem. 

Theorem 641 (Brouwer) A continuous self-map f : K ! K de…ned on a convex compact subset K of R[n] has a …xed point. 

> 3 It is named after Luitzen Brouwer, who proved it in 1912. 

CHAPTER 14. EQUATIONS AND FIXED POINTS 
Proof We …rst consider the interval case K = [0; 1][n] . Let I : [0; 1][n] ! [0; 1][n] be the identity function I (x) = x. We have Ii (0i; x�i) � fi (0i; x�i) and Ii (1i; x�i) � fi (1i; x�i) for all x 2 [0; 1][n] , where 1 = (1; :::; 1). So, we can apply the Poincaré-Miranda’s Theorem to the � function I � f , which ensures the existence of a vector x 2 [0; 1][n] such that (I � f ) (�x) = 0. � Hence, f (�x) = x. The interval case is thus an immediate consequence of Poincaré-Miranda’s Theorem. To consider a general K we need a claim involving its dimension m = dim K � n (see Section 16.6 below). 

Claim There exists a continuous bijection h from K to the closed unit ball B1 (0) of R[m] . 

Proof of the Claim We refer to Stoer and Witzgall (1970) pp. 124 for a proof. Here we just remark that when m = n, i.e., when K has a nonempty interior (Proposition 849 below) and when 0 2 int K, the function h : K ! B1 (0) de…ned by 

is the sought-after continuous bijection from K to B1 (0). 

By Corollary 619, also the inverse function h[�][1] : B1 (0) ! K is continuous. Clearly, a similar bijection exists between B1 (0) and [0; 1][m] . Using compositions, it is then easy to see that there exists a continuous bijection g : [0; 1][m] ! K with continuous inverse g[�][1] : K ! [0; 1][m] . The map 

� is continuous and so, by what previously proved, it has a …xed point x. Since 

we conclude that g (�x) is a …xed point of f , as desired. 

Brouwer’s Theorem is a powerful result that only requires the self-map to be continuous. However, it is demanding on the domain, which has to be a compact and convex set, and it is a non-constructive existence result: it ensures the existence of a …xed point, but gives no information on how to …nd it.[4] 

We close by observing that, as proved by Carlo Miranda in his seminal 1940 piece, Brouwer’s Theorem in turn can be used to prove the Poincaré-Miranda’s Theorem. The two results thus imply each other and, in this sense, are equivalent.[5]

Nice on paper, but in practice it might well not be an easy task to carry out. Remarkably, however, this approach works very well to establish the existence of market equilibria. A remarkable application of Brouwer’s Fixed Point Theorem is the resolution of this more general market equation. We assume that:

Orbits solve the recurrence (14.14) if they can be described in closed form, as it is the case for the recurrences of the last two examples. Unfortunately, often this is not possible and so the main interest of (14.18) is theoretical. Yet, operationally it may still suggest a qualitative analysis of the recurrence. A main issue in this regard is the asymptotic behavior of orbits: where do they end up eventually? for instance, do they converge? 

The next simple, yet important, result shows that …xed points play a key role in studying the convergence of orbits. 

Theorem 649 Let ' : A ! A be a continuous self-map and x0 a point of A. If the orbit f'[n] (x0)g converges to x� 2 A, then x� is a …xed point of '. 

Proof Assume that xn = '[n] (x0) ! x� 2 A. Since ' is continuous, we have ' (�x) = lim ' ('[n] (x0)). So, 

where the equality lim xn+1 = lim xn holds because, as easily checked, if xn ! x then xn+k ! x for every given k � 1. We conclude that x� is a …xed point, as desired. � 

So, a necessary condition for a point to be the limit of a sequence de…ned by a recurrence of order 1 is to be a …xed point of the underlying self-map. If there are no …xed points, convergence is hopeless. If they exist (e.g., by Brouwer’s Theorem), we have some hope. Yet, it is only a necessary condition: as it will become clear later in the section, there are …xed points of ' that are not limit points of the recurrence (14.14).[9] 

Fixed points thus provide the candidate limit points. We have the following procedure to study limits of sequences de…ned by a recurrence (14.14): 

1. Find the collection fx� 2 A : ' (�x) = x�g of the …xed points of the self-map '. 

2. Check whether they are limits of the orbits f'[n] (x0)g, that is, whether '[n] (x0) ! x�. 

> 9 See the oscillating case in Section 14.2.3. 

14.2. ASYMPTOTIC BEHAVIOR OF RECURRENCES 
This procedure is especially e¤ective when …xed points are unique. Indeed, in this case there is a unique candidate limit point for all possible initial conditions x0 2 A, so if orbits converge –e.g., they form a monotone sequence, so Theorem 326 applies –then they have to converge to the …xed point. Remarkably, in this case iterations swamp the initial condition, which asymptotically plays no role in the behavior of the recursion. Regardless of how it starts, the recursion eventually behaves the same. 

In view of this discussion, the next result is especially interesting.[10] 

Proposition 650 If the self-map ' : A ! A is a contraction, then it has at most a unique …xed point.

Rational expectations As we already remarked, given a sequence of equilibrium prices fptg and of price expectations fEt�1 (pt)g, the error forecast et at each t is given by 

The expectation underestimates price pt if et > 0 and overestimates it if et < 0. Instead, if et = 0 the expectation is correct. 

It is plausible that rational producers do not err systematically: errare humanum est, perseverare diabolicum. An extreme form of this principle requires expectations to be always correct: 

It is the so-called hypothesis of rational expectations (or perfect foresight). Though extreme, it is a clear-cut hypothesis that is important to …x ideas. 

In view of the equilibrium relation (8.19), in the market of potatoes with production delays the producers’error forecast et at time t is 

In particular, at each t � 1 one has

We have thus proved the following result. 

14.2. ASYMPTOTIC BEHAVIOR OF RECURRENCES 
Proposition 655 A uniperiodal market equilibrium of markets MRt features rational expectations if and only if the sequence of equilibrium prices is constant with 

for all t � 1. 

The constancy of equilibrium prices is thus equivalent to the correctness of expectations. A non-trivial price dynamics is, thus, the outcome of forecast errors. This result holds for any kind of expectations, extrapolative or not. Indeed, the rationality of expectations is a property of expectations, not an hypothesis on how they are formed: once a possible expectation formation mechanism is speci…ed, a theoretical issue is to understand when they are correct. For instance, in the previous case � = �, we saw that classic expectations are rational if and only if the initial expectation is correct, that is, E0 (p1) = p1. 

The uniperiodal price equilibrium under rational expectations of markets MRt with production delays is equal to the equilibrium price (8.17) of market M . Remarkably, rational expectations have neutralized, in equilibrium, any e¤ect of di¤erences in production technologies. In terms of potatoes’ equilibrium prices, it is immaterial to have a traditional technology, with sowing in t � 1 and harvest in t, rather than a Star Trek one with instantaneous production.

A natural question is whether under adaptive expectations equilibrium prices converge in the long run to the rational expectation equilibrium price, as for instance happens under classic expectations when �< �. ## 14.2.4 Heron’s method 

While computing the square a[2] of a number a is quite simple, the procedure required to compute the square root[p] a of a positive number a is signi…cantly harder. Fortunately, we can count on Heron’s method, a powerful algorithm also known as “Babylonian method”. Given 0 < a 6= 1, Heron’s sequence fxng is de…ned by recurrence by setting x1 = a and 

Thus, Heron’s sequence converges to the square root of a. On top of that, the rate of convergence is quite fast, as we will see in a few examples. 

Proof By induction, it is immediate to show that xn > 0 for all n � 1. Heron’s sequence is convergent because it is (strictly) decreasing, at least after n = 2. To prove it, we …rst observe that

The geometric intuition behind Heron’s method is elegant. It is based on a sequence of rectangles of area equal to a that converge to a square with sides of length[p] a (thus with area a). ## Linear functions and operators 

Earlier in the book, in Chapter 3 we studied at length the linear structure of R[n] . In this chapter we turn to linear functions, an all-important family of functions de…ned on R[n] that preserve its linear structure.

Next we introduce the natural ambient where to carry out these operations over linear functions. 

De…nition 668 The set of all linear functions f : R[n] ! R is called the dual space of R[n] and is denoted by (R[n] )[0] .

The next important result, an elementary version of the celebrated Riesz’s Theorem, describes the dual space (R[n] )[0] . We saw that every vector � 2 R[n] induces a linear function

## 15.1.3 Continuity 

Linearity is a purely algebraic property that requires functions to have a consistent behavior under the operations of addition and scalar multiplication. Thus, prima facie, linearity has no topological consequences.[4] It is, therefore, remarkable that linear functions turn out to be continuous, a most important property as we learned in the last chapter. 

> 4 An algebraic property is about vectors’and operators’manipulations, in particular their addition and scalar multiplication. A topological property is about their proximity, in principle a distinct domain of analysis. Any connection between algebraic and topological properties is thus noteworthy. 

CHAPTER 15. LINEAR FUNCTIONS AND OPERATORS 
Theorem 671 Linear functions are continuous. 

This elegant result is a consequence of the Riesz Theorem, along with the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.

## 15.1.4 Monotonicity 

Turn now to the order structure of R[n] . A function f : R[n] ! R is said to be: 

(i) positive if f (x) � 0 for each x � 0;[5] 

(ii) strictly positive if f (x) > 0 for each x > 0; 

(iii) strongly positive if positive and f (x) > 0 for each x � 0. 

In words, a (strictly, strongly) positive function f assigns (strictly, strongly) positive values f (x) to (strictly, strongly) positive vectors x. 

In general, positivity and increasing monotonicity are altogether independent properties for functions. For instance, f (x) = 1+sin x is positive but not increasing, while g (x) = �e[�][x] is increasing but not positive. Remarkably, for linear functions these two properties become equivalent. 

Proposition 672 A linear function f : R[n] ! R is (strictly, strongly) increasing if and only if it is (strictly, strongly) positive. 

For linear functions, monotonicity thus reduces to positivity, an easier property to check. 

Proof We prove only the increasing part as the proof of the strict and strong versions is similar. “Only if”. Let f : R[n] ! R be linear and increasing. Take any x � 0. We want to show that f (x) � 0. As f is increasing, we have

Intuitively, M (m; n) is another example of a vector space. Note that the neutral element for the addition is the null matrix. 

## 15.2.3 A …rst taxonomy 

Square matrices are particularly important. The main (or principal ) diagonal of a square matrix consists of the elements aii, with i = j. A square matrix is: 

- (i) symmetric when the two triangles separated by the main diagonal are mirror images of each other, that is, aij = aji for all i; j = 1; 2; :::; n. 

- (ii) lower triangular when all the elements above the main diagonal are zero, that is, aij = 0 for all i < j; 

- (iii) upper triangular when all the elements below the main diagonal are zero, that is, aij = 0 for all i > j; 

- (iv) diagonal when it is both lower and upper triangular, so when all the elements outside the main diagonal are zero, that is, aij = 0 for all i 6= j. 

Example 680 The matrix 

is symmetric. The matrices 

are lower triangular, upper triangular and diagonal, respectively. 

We call transpose of a matrix A 2 M (m; n), the matrix B 2 M (n; m) obtained by interchanging the rows and the columns of A, that is, 

for every i = 1; 2; ��� ; n and every j = 1; 2; :::; m. The transpose of A is denoted by A[T] . 

15.2. MATRICES 
Example 681 We have: 

as well as 

Clearly, 

The “transpose of the transpose”of a matrix is thus the matrix itself. In particular, it is easy to see that a square matrix A is symmetric if and only if A[T] = A. In this case, transposition has no e¤ect. Finally, in terms of operations we have 

for all � 2 R and all A; B 2 M (m; n). So, transposition preserves addition and scalar multiplication. 

A row vector x = (x1; :::; xn) 2 R[n] can be regarded as a 1 � n matrix, so we can identify R[n] with M (1; n). According to this identi…cation, the transpose x[T] of x is the column vector 

that is, x[T] 2 M (n; 1). This allows us to identify R[n] also with M (n; 1). With this, in what follows we will often identify the vectors of R[n] with matrices. Sometimes it will convenient to regard them as row vectors, so as elements of M (1; n), sometimes as column vectors, so as elements of M (n; 1). Be that as it may, one should not forget that vectors are elements of R[n] , identi…cations are holograms. 

## 15.2.4 Matrix multiplication 

It is possible to multiply two matrices A and B under suitable conditions on their dimensions. We …rst present the special case of the multiplication of a matrix with a vector. So, let 

The choice of the dimensions of A and x is not arbitrary: the multiplication between a matrix A and a column vector x[T] requires the number of rows of x to be equal to the number of 

CHAPTER 15. LINEAR FUNCTIONS AND OPERATORS 
columns of A. If this is the case, their product Ax[T] is de…ned by 

Here a[1] , a[2] , ..., a[m] are the rows of A and 

are the inner products between the rows of A and the vector x. In particular, Ax[T] 2 M (m; 1). It is thus evident why the dimension of the vector x must be equal to the number of columns of A: in multiplying A with x, the components of Ax[T] are the inner products between the rows of A and the vector x. But, inner products are possible only between vectors of the same dimension. 

Notation To ease notation, in what follows we will just write Ax instead of Ax[T] .

Matrix multiplication has the following properties, as the reader can verify. 

Proposition 685 Let A; B and C be any three matrices for which it is possible to take the products indicated below. Then, 

Among the properties of matrix multiplication, commutativity is missing. Indeed, this operation does not satisfy this property: when both products AB and BA are well de…ned, in general we have AB = BA. The next example will illustrate this notable failure. A piece of terminology: when AB = BA, we say that the two matrices commute. Since (AB)[T] = B[T] A[T] , two matrices commute if and only if their transposes commute.

## 15.3 Linear operators

We now turn to some basic properties of linear operators that generalize properties that we established for linear functions. A …rst important property, which generalizes Proposition 664, is the preservation of linear combinations.

As already discussed for linear functions, property (15.11) has the important consequence that, once we know the values taken by a linear operator T on the elements of a basis of R[n] , we can determine the values of T for each vector of R[n] . 

Not surprisingly, linear operators keep passing through the origin (as the reader can check by adapting the proof of Proposition 665).

the space of all linear operators T : R[n] ! R[m] . In the case of linear functions, i.e., m = 1, the space L (R[n] ; R) reduces to the dual space (R[n] )[0] that we studied before. It is immediate to check that addition and scalar multiplication preserve linearity: 

(ii) if T 2 L (R[n] ; R[m] ) and � 2 R, then �T 2 L (R[n] ; R[m] ). 

The space L (R[n] ; R[m] ) is thus closed under these two operations, which are also easily seen to satisfy the “usual” properties (v1)-(v8). Again, this means that L (R[n] ; R[m] ) is, intuitively, another example of a vector space. To ease notation we just write L (R[n] ) in place of L (R[n] ; R[n] ) in the special case n = m, i.e., for linear operators T : R[n] ! R[n] having the same domain and codomain. 

Addition and scalar multiplication are, by now, routine operations. The next operation of multiplication is, instead, peculiar to operators. 

De…nition 696 Given two linear operators T : R[n] ! R[m] and S : R[m] ! R[q] , their product is the function ST : R[n] ! R[q] de…ned by 

for every x 2 R[n] . 

In other words, the product operator ST is the composite function S � T . If the operators S and T are linear, also the product ST is so. Indeed: 

for every x; y 2 R[n] and every �; � 2 R. The product of two linear operators is, therefore, still a linear operator. 

As Proposition 703 will make clear momentarily, the multiplication of linear operators is in general not a commutative operation: when both products ST and TS are de…ned, we might well have ST = TS. Hence, when one writes ST and TS. The order with which the two operators appear is important. 

Finally, we extend to operators the remarkable continuity property of linear functions (Theorem 671). 

## Proposition 697 Linear operators are continuous. 

The proof is similar, mutatis mutandis, to that of the earlier Theorem 671, as the reader can check.

## 15.3.2 Representation 

In this section we study more in detail linear operators T : R[n] ! R[m] by establishing a representation theorem for them. In Riesz’s Theorem we saw that a function f : R[n] ! R is linear if and only if there exists a vector � 2 R[n] such that f (x) = � � x for every x 2 R[n] . Next we generalize this all-important result to linear operators. 

Theorem 698 (Operator Riesz Theorem) An operator T : R[n] ! R[m] is linear if and only if there exists a unique matrix mA�n[such][that] 

The matrix A is called matrix associated to the operator T (or also representative matrix of the operator T ). It reduces to the vector � 2 R[n] of Riesz’s Theorem, which is the special case of this theorem when m = 1.

where a[1] , a[2] , ..., a[m] are the rows of A.

It is hard to overestimate the importance of the Operator Riesz Theorem. It establishes a one-to-one correspondence between linear operator and matrices, which can be diagrammed as follows: 

When m = 1, we go back to (15.6). This correspondence permits to identify the space L (R[n] ; R[m] ) with the space of matrices M (m; n). 

Matrices allow us, therefore, to represent operators in the form (15.12), something of great importance both theoretically and operationally. This is why matrices are so important: though the fundamental notion is that of operator, thanks to the representation (15.12) matrices become a most useful auxiliary notion that will accompany us in the rest of the book. 

We illustrate all this with a couple of examples.

## 15.3.3 Matrices and operations

As we saw in Section 15.2.4, the multiplication of matrices is in general not commutative, a failure that indeed re‡ects the lack of commutativity of the multiplication of linear operators, a very important aspect of the theory of linear operators. 

Finally, in the infracoda we introduce the operators having transposes of matrices as their representative matrices. Though matrix transposition is a very simple notion, its operator rendering is less so and for this reason it is coda material. 

## 15.4 Rank 

## 15.4.1 Linear operators 

The kernel, denoted ker T , of an operator T : R[n] ! R[m] is the set 

It is thus the preimage of 0 under T , i.e., 

In words, the kernel is the set of all points where the operator takes on a null value (i.e., the zero vector of R[m] ). When the operator T is linear, its kernel is always not empty because 0 2 ker T as T (0) = 0 by Proposition 695. 

15.4. RANK 
Example 704 De…ne T : R[2] ! R[2] by 

This operator is easily seen to be linear. We have ker T = f0g, i.e., the zero vector is the only vector where T takes on value 0. Indeed, a vector x = (x1; x2) of the plane belongs to ker T when both the di¤erence and the sum of its components is null, a condition that only the vector 0 satis…es. N 

Another important set is the image (or range) of T , de…ned in the usual way as 

The image is, therefore, the set of the vectors of R[m] that are “reached” from R[n] through the operator T . 

For linear operators these two sets turn out to be vector subspaces. 

Lemma 705 If T : R[n] ! R[m] is linear, then ker T is a vector subspace of R[n] and Im T is a vector subspace of R[m] . 

Proof We show the result for ker T , leaving Im T to the reader. Let x; y 2 ker T , i.e., T (x) = 0 and T (y) = 0. For every �; � 2 R, we have 

Thus, �x + �y 2 ker T and this proves that ker T is a vector subspace of R[n] . � 

These vector subspaces are important when dealing with the properties of injectivity and surjectivity of linear operators. In particular, by de…nition the operator T is surjective when Im T = R[m] , that is, when the subspace Im T coincides with the entire space R[m] . As to injectivity, by exploiting the linearity of T we have the following simple kernel characterization. 

Lemma 706 A linear operator T : R[n] ! R[m] is injective if and only if ker T = f0g. 

Thus, the linear operator (15.15) is injective. 

Proof “If”. Suppose that ker T = f0g. We want to show that T is injective. Let x; y 2 R[n] with x = y. Since x � y = 0, the hypothesis ker T = f0g implies x � y 2= ker T , i.e., T (x � y) 6= 0. By the linearity of T , we then have T (x) 6= T (y). “Only if”. Let T : R[n] ! R[m] be an injective linear operator. We want to show that ker T = f0g. By Proposition 695, T (0) = 0 and so 0 2 ker T . Let 0 = x 2 R[n] . By injectivity, T (x) 6= T (0) = 0 and so x 2= ker T . We conclude that ker T = f0g. � 

We can now state the important Rank-Nullity Theorem, which says that the dimension n of the Euclidean space R[n] is the sum of the dimensions of the two subspaces ker T and Im T determined by a linear operator T . To this end, we give a name to such dimensions.

Corollary 710 Let T : R[n] ! R[m] be linear.

CHAPTER 15. LINEAR FUNCTIONS AND OPERATORS 
For a generic function, injectivity and surjectivity are altogether distinct and independent properties: for instance, just by looking at their graphs it is immediate to see that f (x) = arctan x is injective but not surjective, while f (x) = x sin x is surjective but not injective. The next important result, a remarkable consequence of the Rank-Nullity Theorem, shows that for linear operators T : R[n] ! R[n] with the same domain and codomain the two properties turn out to be, instead, equivalent. 

Corollary 711 A linear operator T : R[n] ! R[n] is injective if and only if it is surjective. 

Proof “If”. Let T be surjective, i.e., Im T = R[n] . By (15.17), 

Hence, � (T ) = 0. In turn, this implies that ker T = f0g. By Lemma 706, T is injective. “Only if”. Let T be injective. By Lemma 706, ker T = f0g. By (15.17), 

Hence, � (T ) = n. As Im T � R[n] , in turn this implies that Im T = R[n] . We conclude that T is surjective. � 

Example 712 The injective linear operator (15.15) is thus surjective as well, so it is bijective. N 

Remarkably, for a linear operator T : R[n] ! R[n] the following properties are thus equivalent: 

(i) T is bijective; 

(ii) T is injective, i.e., ker T = f0g; 

(iii) T is surjective, i.e., Im T = R[n] . 

An equivalent way to state this equivalence is to say that the following conditions are equivalent: 

(i) T is bijective; 

(ii) � (T ) = 0; 

(iii) � (T ) = n. 

## 15.4.2 Rank of matrices 

The rank of a matrix is one of the central notions of linear algebra. 

De…nition 713 The rank of a matrix A, denoted by � (A), is the maximum number of its linearly independent columns. 

We illustrate this notion with an example. 

15.4. RANK 
Example 714 Let 

Since the third column can be obtained by multiplying the second column by 3, the set of all four columns is linearly dependent. Therefore, � (A) < 4. Instead, it is easy to verify that the …rst, second, and fourth columns are linearly independent, like the …rst, third, and fourth columns. Thus, � (A) = 3. Note that there are two di¤erent sets of linearly independent columns, with the same cardinality. N 

Let A be the matrix associated to a linear operator T . Since the vector subspace Im T is generated by the column vectors of A,[11] we have � (T ) � � (A) (why?). The next result shows that, actually, equality holds: the notions of rank for operators and for matrices are consistent. 

## Proposition 715 A linear operator and its associated matrix have the same rank.

It follows that 

Thanks to the Rank-Nullity Theorem, this proposition has the following corollary showing that the linear independence of the columns is the matrix counterpart of injectivity. 

Corollary 716 A linear operator is injective if and only if the columns of its associated matrix are linearly independent.

So far we have considered the linear independence of the columns of A. The connection with the linear independence of the rows of A is, however, very tight as the next important consistency result shows. In reading it, note that the rank of the transpose matrix A[T] is, by de…nition, the maximum number of linearly independent rows of A. 

Theorem 717 For every matrix A the maximum numbers of its linearly independent rows and columns coincide, i.e., � (A) = �(A[T] ).

Example 718 Consider the rows of the matrix 

Since the …rst row is obtained by multiplying the second one by 3, the set of all the three rows is linearly dependent. Therefore, �(A[T] ) < 3. Instead, the two rows (3; 6; 18) and (0; 1; 3) are linearly independent, like the rows (1; 2; 6) and (0; 1; 3). Therefore, �(A[T] ) = 2. N 

The maximum sets of linearly independent rows or columns can be di¤erent: in the matrix of the last example we have two di¤erent sets, both for the rows and for the columns. Yet, they have the same cardinality because � (A) = �(A[T] ). It is a remarkable consistency property that, in view of Corollary 711, shows that for a linear operator T : R[n] ! R[n] the following conditions are equivalent: 

- (i) T is injective; 

- (ii) T is surjective; 

- (iii) the columns of A are linearly independent, that is, � (A) = n; 

- (iv) the rows of A are linearly independent, that is, �(A[T] ) = n. 

The equivalence of these conditions is one of the deepest results of linear algebra. 

## 15.4.3 Properties 

From Theorem 717 it follows that when A 2 M (m; n) we have 

If it happens that � (A) = min fm; ng, the matrix A is said to be of full rank as the rank cannot assume a higher value. 

The next result gathers some properties of the rank. 

Proposition 719 Let A; B 2 M (m; n). Then, 

- (i) � (A + B) � � (A) + � (B) and � (�A) = � (A) for every � = 0; 

CHAPTER 15. LINEAR FUNCTIONS AND OPERATORS 
(ii) � (A) = � (CA) = � (AD) = � (CAD) if C and D are square matrices of full rank;[12] (iii) � (A) = �(A[T] A). 

Point (i) shows the behavior of the rank under the matrix operations of addition and scalar multiplication. Points (ii) and (iii) are interesting properties of invariance of the rank under the product of matrices. The square matrix 

is called Gram matrix and plays an important role in applications (for instance, we will meet it in connection with the least squares method). 

Clearly, the rank of a matrix does not change if one permutes the places of two columns. So, it is without loss of generality to assume that the …rst r columns of a matrix A of rank r are linearly independent. This useful convention will be used several times, beginning with the next proof.

## 15.4.4 Gaussian elimination procedure 

The Gaussian elimination procedure is an important algorithm for the calculation of the rank of matrices. Another algorithm, due to Kronecker, will be presented later in the chapter once armed with the notion of determinant. 

We start with a simple observation. There are matrices that reveal immediately their rank. For example, both matrices 

have rank 3: in matrix A the …rst three columns are linearly independent (they are the three versors of R[3] ); in matrix B the …rst three rows are linearly independent. These two matrices (15.25) are a special case of echelon matrices. Speci…cally, matrix A is echelon if:[13] 

- (i) the non-zero rows of A have 1 as their …rst non-zero component (called pivot); 

- (ii) the other elements of the pivot’s column are zero; 

- (iii) pivots form a “little scale”from the left to the right: a pivot of a lower row is to the right of the pivot of an upper row; 

- (iv) the zero rows (if they exist) lie under the other rows, so in the lower part of the matrix. 

Example 720 (i) The matrix 

is echelon, with pivots in boldface. 

(ii) A square matrix is echelon when it is diagonal, possibly followed by zero rows. For example: 

> 13 To ease exposition, we call “zero row”a row with only zero elements. 

N 

15.4. RANK 
Clearly, the non-zero rows of an echelon matrix are linearly independent. The rank of an echelon matrix is, therefore, obvious: 

## Lemma 721 The rank of an echelon matrix is equal to the number of non-zero rows. 

There exist some simple operations that permit to transform any matrix into an echelon matrix. Such operations, called elementary operations (by row ),[14] are: 

- (i) operation E1: multiply any row by a non-zero scalar; 

- (ii) operation E2: add to any row a multiple of any other row; 

- (iii) operation E3: interchange any two rows. 

These three operations amounts to multiplying on the left the matrix A 2 M (m; n) by suitable m � m square matrices, called elementary. Speci…cally, 

- (i) operation E1: “to multiply the s-th row of A by a scalar �”amounts to multiply on the left A by the elementary matrix 

which coincides with the identity matrix Im except that, in the place (s; s), we have � instead of 1; 

- (ii) operation E2: “to add to the r-th row of A a multiple � of the s-th row”amounts to multiply on the left A by the elementary matrix 

which coincides with the identity matrix Im except that, in the place (r; s), we have � instead of 0; 

- (iii) operation E3: “to interchange the r-th row and the s-th row of A”amounts to multiply on the left A by the elementary matrix 

which coincides with the identity matrix Im except that the r-th row and the s-th row are interchanged.

Example 725 If A is square of order n, its echelon matrix A[�] is an upper triangular square matrix of order n, with diagonal composed of only 1s and 0s (recall Example 720). In particular, if A has full rank, then A[�] is the identity matrix In of order n: N 

Couple of …nal remarks. 

- (i) Each matrix has a unique echelon matrix, but not vice versa: di¤erent matrices may happen to be transformed into the same echelon matrix. For instance, all square matrices of full rank gets transformed in the identity matrix (cf. Example 725). Of course, this is of no concern computationally as what matters is moving from A to A[�] , not vice versa. 

- (ii) To establish whether k vectors x[1] ; x[2] ; :::; x[k] 2 R[n] are linearly independent, with k � n (otherwise the answer is certainly negative) we can construct the n � k matrix that has these vectors as columns. They are linearly independent if and only if the rank of this matrix is k. We can thus use the Gaussian elimination procedure just studied to compute the rank of this auxiliary matrix.

In the last section we saw a …rst characterization of the invertibility through the notions of rank and nullity (Corollary 711). We give now another characterization of invertibility showing that the existence of either a right or a left inverse grants full-‡edged invertibility. 

Proposition 729 For an operator T 2 L (R[n] ) the following conditions are equivalent: 

- (i) T is invertible; 

- (ii) there exists S 2 L (R[n] ) such that TS = I; 

- (iii) there exists R 2 L (R[n] ) such that RT = I.

CHAPTER 15. LINEAR FUNCTIONS AND OPERATORS 
Proof (i) implies (ii). Since T is invertible, it is enough to set S = T[�][1] . 

(ii) implies (iii). Assume TS = I. We …rst show that ker S = f0g. By contradiction, assume that there exists x = 0 such that S (x) = 0. Since T is linear, we reach the contradiction 0 = T (0) = T (S (x)) = I (x) = x. Hence, ker S = f0g and so the linear operator S is injective (Lemma 706). By Corollary 711, we conclude that S is bijective. We are left to show that ker T = f0g. By contradiction, assume that there exists x = 0 such that T (x) = 0. Since S is bijective, there exists y 2 R[n] such that S (y) = x, with y = 0. Since TS = I, we have y = T (S (y)) = T (x) = 0, a contradiction. Hence, ker T = f0g and so the linear operator T is injective (Lemma 706). By Corollary 711, we conclude that T is bijective. To complete the proof, it is enough to set R = T[�][1] . 

(iii) implies (ii). RT = I. By proceeding as in the last argument we can show that T is invertible. 

Finally, let TS = I. We just proved that T is invertible. Then, T[�][1] (TS) = T[�][1] , yielding that S = T[�][1] . Similarly, from RT = I it follows that R = T[�][1] . �

In these examples we computed the determinant of a square matrix using its recursive de…nition, a well speci…ed procedure – an algorithm – based on submatrices. There exist various techniques to simplify the calculation of determinants (we will see some of them shortly). ## 15.6.2 Interpretations 

In this subsection we provide geometric and combinatorial angles on determinants. In the combinatorial one we introduce the important Leibniz formula. 

CHAPTER 15. LINEAR FUNCTIONS AND OPERATORS 
## Geometry 

Geometrically, the determinant of a square matrix measures (with a sign!) the “space taken up”by its column vectors. To see why, at least in the simplest case, take a 2 � 2 matrix 

with a11 > a12 > 0 and a22 > a21 > 0 (the other possibilities can be similarly studied, as readers can check). In the following …gure 

the determinant of A is represented by the area of the parallelogram OBGC, which is twice the area of the triangle OBC obtained from the two columns of A. The area of the triangle OBC can be easily calculated by subtracting from the area of the rectangle ODEF the areas of the three triangles ODB, OCF , and BEC. Since 

one gets 

Therefore,

- (i) when we exchange the two columns, the determinant changes only its sign (because the parallelogram is covered in the opposite direction);

- (ii) when the two vectors are proportional –that is, linearly dependent –the determinant is zero (because the parallelogram collapses to a segment).

## Combinatorics 

A permutation of the set of numbers N = f1; 2; :::; ng is represented by a bijection � : N ! N , as detailed in Appendix B.2. There are n! possible permutations on N . Let � be the set of all them. We have an inversion in a permutation � 2 � if, for some k; k[0] 2 N , 

We say that the permutation is odd (resp., even) if it features an odd (resp., even) number of inversions. The function sgn : � ! f�1; 1g de…ned by 

is called parity.[17] In particular, an even permutation has parity +1, while an odd permutation has parity �1.

This formula is sometimes called Leibniz formula for the determinant. Observe that each term in the sum (15.31) contains only one element of each row and only one element of each column. This will be crucial in the proofs of the next section. 

## 15.6.3 Properties 

We now turn to the remarkable properties of determinants, which we often prove via the Leibniz formula, the deus ex machina of this part. It is …tting to begin, of course, with linearity. The determinant per se is not linear: for example, when n > 1, 

Indeed, in view of Example 737 we have det In + In = det 2In = 2[n] = 2 = det In + det In. 

Yet, the determinant has the following ceteris paribus linearity property, with all the action happening within a single raw, all else being kept equal. As usual, a[i] denotes a generic raw of a matrix A. 

Proposition 741 Let A be a square matrix of order n. For each raw i it holds 

for all x; y 2 R[n] and all �; � 2 R. 

15.6. DETERMINANTS 
In words, if raw i of a square matrix is a linear combination of two vectors x and y, then its determinant is the linear combination of the determinants of the two matrices featuring the same raw i equal to x and to y, respectively. This raw-by-raw linearity extends to columns, as the reader can check. It is sometimes called the multilinearity of the determinant.

15.6. DETERMINANTS 
Thus, the linear operator D : R[3] ! R[3] is 

The next proposition collects some further important properties of determinants that, along with its multilinearity, are also useful for their computation. In the statement “line” stands for either row or column: the properties hold, indeed, symmetrically for both the rows and the columns of the matrix. Accordingly, by “parallel lines” we mean either two rows or two columns. 

Proposition 744 Let A and B be two square matrices of the same order. 

(i) If a line of A is zero, then det A = 0. 

(ii) If B is obtained from A by multiplying a line by a scalar k, then det B = k det A. 

(iii) If B is obtained from A by interchanging two parallel lines, then det B = � det A. 

- (iv) If two parallel lines of A are equal, then det A = 0. 

Proof The proof relies on the Leibniz formula for the determinant, in particular on the fact that, as previously observed, each term in that formula contains exactly one element of each row and one element of each column. In the proof we consider only rows (similar arguments hold for columns). 

(i) and (ii) In Leibniz’s formula there appears one element of each row: if a row is zero, all the products are then zero. Similarly, all the products turn out to be multiplied by k when a raw is multiplied by k. 

(iii) By exchanging two rows, all the even permutations become odd and vice versa. By Leibniz’s formula, the determinant changes sign. 

(iv) Let A be the matrix that has equal rows i and j and let A[ij] be the matrix with such rows interchanged. By (iii), we have det A[ij] = � det A. But, being the two interchanged rows equal, we have A = A[ij] . So, det A[ij] = det A. Thus, det A = � det A and so det A = 0. � 

As a consequence of the last two results we get a useful invariance property of determinants. 

Corollary 745 Let A and B be two square matrices of the same order. If B is obtained from A by adding to a line a multiple of a parallel line, then det B = det A. 

Proof Denote again by a[i] a generic raw of matrix A. The matrix obtained from A by adding, for example, k times the …rst row to the second one is 

CHAPTER 15. LINEAR FUNCTIONS AND OPERATORS 
Let 

By the multilinearity of the determinant, det B = det A + det C. By Proposition 744-(ii), det C = k det D. Since D has two equal rows, by Proposition 744-(i) we have det D = 0. We conclude that det B = det A. � 

We can now tell how the elementary operations E1-E3 of the Gaussian elimination procedure modify the determinant of A. Speci…cally: 

- E1: if B is obtained from A by multiplying a row of the matrix A by a constant � = 0, then det B = � det A by Proposition 744-(ii); 

- E2: if B is obtained from A by adding to a row of A the multiple of another row, then det B = det A by Corollary 745; 

- E3: if B is obtained from A by exchanging two rows of A, then det B = � det A by Proposition 744-(iii). 

In particular, when matrix B is obtained from A via elementary operations, we have 

This observation leads to the following important characterization of square matrices of full rank. 

Proposition 746 A square matrix A has full rank if and only if det A 6= 0. 

Proof “Only if”. If A has full rank, its unique echelon square matrix of full rank is the identity matrix In (cf. Example 725). As det In = 1, by (15.32) we conclude that det A 6= 0. “If”. Let det A = 0. Suppose, by contradiction, that A does not have full rank. Then, its rows are not linearly independent (Corollary 731) and so at least one of them is a linear combination of the others. Such row can be made 0 by repeatedly adding to it carefully chosen multiples of the other rows. Denote by B such transformed matrix. By Proposition 744-(i), det B = 0. By (15.32) we then reach the contradiction det A = 0. We conclude that A has full rank. � 

This result has, along with Corollary 731, the following immediate but noteworthy consequence. 

Corollary 747 For a square matrix A the following conditions are equivalent: 

- (i) the rows are linearly independent; 

- (ii) the columns are linearly independent; 

- (iii) det A 6= 0. 

15.6. DETERMINANTS 
The determinants behave well with respect to the product, as the next result shows. It is a key property of determinants.

## 15.6.6 Ranks and determinants: Kronecker’s Algorithm 

There is an interesting determinant angle on the rank of a matrix. To see it, recall some previously proved results: 

1. if the rank of a matrix is r, it contains at most r linearly independent columns (so, also r linearly independent rows); 

2. r vectors x[1] ; x[2] ; :::; x[r] of R[r] are linearly independent if and only if the determinant of the square matrix of order r that has them as rows (or columns) is non-zero; 

3. if r vectors x[1] ; x[2] ; :::; x[r] of R[r] are linearly independent in R[r] , then the r vectors y[1] ; y[2] ; :::; y[r] of R[n] , with n > r, that have exactly x[1] ; x[2] ; :::; x[r] as their …rst r components are linearly independent in R[n] .[18] 

These basic results underlie (why?) the following result. 

Proposition 760 A matrix A has rank r if and only if it has a non-singular square submatrix of order r and all its square submatrix of order r + 1 (if there exist any) are singular. 

We call minor of order r the determinant of a square submatrix of A of order r. Under this standard terminology, we can say that the rank of a matrix is the maximum order of its non-zero minors. 

Example 761 Let[19] 

All its minors of order 3 are zero: 

On the other hand, we have the following non-zero minor of order 2: 

By the last proposition, the rank of A is 2. 

> 18 The property is easy to verify and has already been used in the proof of Proposition 719. To exemplify, take r = 2 and n = 3. Since the bidimensional vectors x[1] = (0; 1) and x[2] = (1; 0) of R[2] are linearly independent, the three-dimensional vectors y[1] = (0; 1; 5) and y[2] = (1; 0; 10) of R[3] having them as their …rst two components are also linearly independent. 

> 19 This example is from Mirsky (1955) p. 136. 

15.6. DETERMINANTS 
This result sheds some further light on the nature of the rank of a matrix, but the next version is more useful to compute it as it involves fewer minors. 

Proposition 762 A matrix A has rank r if and only if it has a non-singular square submatrix B of order r such that each square submatrix C of order r + 1 (if there exist any) that contains B is singular. 

The Kronecker Algorithm uses this result to compute the rank of a matrix through determinants. It proceeds as follows. 

- (i) We choose as “leader”a square submatrix B of order r of A that is readily seen to be not singular; pragmatically, we often take a submatrix of order 2. 

- (ii) We “border”in all the possible ways the submatrix B with one of the surviving rows and one of the surviving columns. If all these submatrices C of order r +1 are singular, the rank of A is r and the procedure ends. Otherwise, if we run into a non-singular submatrix C, we start again by taking it as a new “leader”. 

The Kronecker Algorithm is best described using minors. To this end, given a matrix A, we call bordered minor of a square submatrix B of order r the determinant of a submatrix C of A of order r + 1 that contains B. 

Example 763 Let 

Let us choose as leader the minor 

With the last two columns and the last non-used row, we obtain the following bordered minors: 

So, the rank of A is 2. 

## 15.6.7 Summing up 

We conclude this section by noting how the rank of a matrix is simultaneously many things, each of them being a possible de…nition of it. Indeed, it is: 

- (i) the maximum number of its linearly independent columns; 

- (ii) the maximum number of its linearly independent rows; 

- (iii) the maximum order of its non-zero minors; 

CHAPTER 15. LINEAR FUNCTIONS AND OPERATORS 
- (iv) the dimension of the image of the linear operator that the matrix determines. 

The rank is a multi-faceted notion that plays a key role in linear algebra and its many applications. Operationally, the Gaussian elimination procedure and the Kronecker’s Algorithm permit to compute it. 

## 15.7 Square linear systems 

## 15.7.1 Equations with a vector unknown 

An important application of what we just learned about linear operators from R[n] into R[n] is the solution of a “square” linear system of n equations in n unknowns: 

It can be written in matrix form as: 

where A is the square n � n matrix of the system coe¢ cients 

while 

are the vectors of unknowns and known terms in R[n] , respectively. 

To go deeper into the analysis, consider the linear operator T : R[n] ! R[n] associated to A, de…ned by 

The system (15.40) can be written in operator form as 

This form clari…es the nature of the linear system (15.40): it is an equation in which the unknown is a vector x 2 R[n] and the known term is a vector b 2 R[n] .[20] It is a linear equation because the operator T itself is linear. A solution of the equation is a vector x 2 R[n] such that T (x) = b. The set of all solutions –i.e., the solution set –is thus given by the preimage 

Summing up, a linear system is a linear equation with a vector unknown. The standard terminology “system of equations”should be used cum grano salis. 

> 20 Equations have been studied in Chapter 14. 

15.7. SQUARE LINEAR SYSTEMS 
Example 764 When b = 0, the linear system (15.40) is called homogeneous. In this important case, the solution set is the vector subspace T[�][1] (0) = ker T . When the matrix A is invertible, by Lemma 706 we have ker T = f0g and so there is a unique solution given by the zero vector x = 0. N 

## 15.7.2 Global unique existence: Cramer’s Theorem 

We ask two “global”questions about the linear system (15.40): 

- Existence: is there a solution for every known term b 2 R[n] ? 

- Uniqueness: is such a solution unique? 

To address these questions,[21] observe that a vector x 2 R[n] solves the linear system (15.40) for a given known term b when x 2 T[�][1] (b). In particular, a solution exists when 

that is, when b 2 Im T . Such a solution is unique when T[�][1] (b) is a singleton. Back to the two previous questions, we then have that: 

- a solution exists for every known term b 2 R[n] when T is surjective, that is, Im T = R[n] ; 

- a unique solution exists for every known term b 2 R[n] when T is injective.[22] 

Injectivity and surjectivity are, by Corollary 711, equivalent properties for the linear operator T . Thus, the two problems of existence and uniqueness are equivalent: there exists a solution for the system (15.40) for every known term if and only if such a solution is unique. 

With this, a necessary and su¢ cient condition the existence of a unique solution for every b 2 R[n] is the invertibility of operator T . In matrix terms, this amounts to satisfy any of the following equivalent conditions: 

- (i) the matrix A is invertible; 

- (ii) the matrix A is non-singular, i.e., det A 6= 0; 

- (iii) the matrix A is of full rank, i.e., � (A) = n. 

In particular, the inverse operator T[�][1] : R[n] ! R[n] gives the solution 

for the known term b. Next we sum up all this in Cramer’s Theorem, which is thus a consequence of Corollary 711, so ultimately of the Rank-Nullity Theorem. 

> 21 In Chapter 14 we only considered the existence of solutions of a general equation (not necessarily linear) for a speci…c known term. Here, in the special case of a linear equation, we can be more ambitious and consider universal questions involving all known terms. 

> 22 Recall that a function is injective if and only if all its preimages are singletons. 

CHAPTER 15. LINEAR FUNCTIONS AND OPERATORS 
Theorem 765 (Cramer) Let A be a square matrix of order n. The linear system 

has a unique solution for every b 2 R[n] if and only if the matrix A is invertible. In this case, the solution is given by

As a straightforward corollary of this result we have the following simple characterization of invertible Vandermonde matrices. 

Corollary 770 A Vandermonde matrix A of order n is invertible if and only if its n parameters a1, a2, ..., an are distinct. 

Armed with this result, let us go back to the linear system (15.46). Let the parameters a1, a2, ..., an be distinct, so that the Vandermonde matrix A is invertible. By Cramer’s Theorem, this linear system admits a unique solution x� that, in turn, provides the coe¢ cients of the polynomial
